did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (Custom CNN, Xception, and EfficientNetB0) to perform skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset. The study specifically evaluated the impact of lesion masking on the performance of these AI architectures for dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a comparative study of deep learning models (Custom CNN, Xception, EfficientNetB0) on the HAM10000 dataset to evaluate the impact of lesion masking. It includes specific methodology, quantitative results (accuracy, F1-scores, p-values), and statistical analysis, classifying it as a research paper despite being published in a conference proceedings (PuneCon).

title: Impact of Lesion Masking on Skin Cancer Detection Using Deep Learning: A Comparative Study of CNN, Xception, and EfficientNetB0
brief_summary: This study evaluated the impact of automated lesion masking on the performance of three deep learning architectures—Custom CNN, Xception, and EfficientNetB0—for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers compared model accuracy, macro-F1 scores, and AUC-ROC metrics on masked versus unmasked dermatoscopic images to determine if isolating the lesion region improves diagnostic precision. EfficientNetB0 demonstrated the most significant improvement, with accuracy increasing from 68.43% to 78.02% and macro-F1 rising from 0.48 to 0.73 after masking. In contrast, the high-performing Custom CNN and Xception models showed negligible changes or slight performance drops, likely because they already extracted sufficient features without background noise. Statistical analysis via the Wilcoxon signed-rank test confirmed that the improvements for EfficientNetB0 were significant, while differences for the other models were not. The findings suggest that automated lesion masking is particularly beneficial for mid-range models and can enhance computer-aided skin cancer detection systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven diagnostic categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Classified as a malignant/rare class in the study.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Identified as a common benign class.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Classified as a malignant class.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Classified as a rare malignant class.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Classified as a common benign class.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Classified as a rare class.
One of the seven diagnostic categories in the HAM10000 dataset. Classified as a rare class.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The study performs multi-class classification of dermatoscopic images into seven distinct pathological categories using the HAM10000 dataset. The models (Custom CNN, Xception, EfficientNetB0) output class predictions via softmax layers, and performance is evaluated using accuracy, macro-F1 scores, and AUC-ROC metrics.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, sourced from multiple populations and supplemented with pixel-level expert annotations delineating lesion boundaries. It is part of the ISIC 2018 dataset.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (Custom CNN, Xception, EfficientNetB0) after synthetic upsampling to approximately 4,000 samples per category and augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (e.g., monitoring validation loss).
use.testing: Used for testing model performance (accuracy, F1-score, AUC-ROC) on both masked and unmasked inputs.
use.other: Pixel-level segmentation masks were available for most images to facilitate controlled comparisons of masked versus unmasked classification.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the statistical significance of performance improvements (class-wise F1-scores and AUC scores) between masked and unmasked model conditions.
To determine statistical significance for the Wilcoxon signed-rank test results.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in performance metrics (class-wise F1-scores and AUC scores) between masked and unmasked model conditions.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in performance metrics (class-wise F1-scores and AUC scores) between masked and unmasked model conditions.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study used a significance threshold of p < 0.05. For EfficientNetB0, the improvements in accuracy, macro-F1, and class-wise metrics were statistically significant (p < 0.05 or p < 0.01). For Custom CNN and Xception, the differences in performance were not statistically significant (p > 0.05).

HAM10000.experiment: Comparison of Masked vs Unmasked inputs across three architectures

Custom CNN.statistical_significance: Not significant (p = 0.116 for overall change; p > 0.05 for most class-wise F1/AUC changes)

Xception.statistical_significance: Not significant (p = 0.414 for overall change; p > 0.05 for most class-wise F1/AUC changes)

EfficientNetB0.statistical_significance: Significant (p = 0.016 for overall change; p < 0.05 or p < 0.01 for all class-wise F1 and AUC improvements)

HAM10000.notes: EfficientNetB0 showed the most significant improvement with accuracy increasing from 68.43% to 78.02% and macro-F1 rising from 0.48 to 0.73. Custom CNN and Xception showed negligible changes or slight performance drops. Statistical analysis via Wilcoxon signed-rank test confirmed improvements for EfficientNetB0 were significant, while differences for Custom CNN and Xception were not.

architecture_used.pretraining: Xception and EfficientNetB0 initialized with ImageNet pre-trained weights; Custom CNN was randomly initialized.

Custom CNN.layers: Four convolutional blocks (ReLU activations, max-pooling), followed by dense layers (64 and 32 neurons, ReLU)

Xception.type: Transfer Learning Model using Depthwise Separable Convolutions
Xception.layers: Features flattened, processed via a 256-neuron dense layer (ReLU, 0.5 dropout), and output through a 7-unit softmax

EfficientNetB0.layers: GlobalAveragePooling2D, BatchNormalization, two 0.5 dropout layers around a 512-neuron dense layer (ReLU, L1/L2 regularization 0.01), and a 7-unit softmax output

training_details.augmentation: Synthetic upsampling (~4,000 samples per category), angular perturbations (±20°), horizontal flipping, luminance scaling (0.8–1.2)

preprocessing_techniques: Synthetic upsampling (approx. 4,000 samples per category) to mitigate class imbalance
Resizing/Rescaling to target dimensions (71x71 pixels for Custom CNN and Xception; 224x224 pixels for EfficientNetB0)
Training-time augmentation: Angular perturbations (±20°), horizontal flipping, and luminance scaling (0.8–1.2)
Automated lesion masking via binary mask application (element-wise multiplication of original image and binary segmentation mask) to remove background noise and artifacts
Standardization of input images prior to masking

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to mitigate class imbalance, targeting approximately 4,000 samples per category for underrepresented classes.
Applied during training with a range of ±20 degrees.
Applied as a geometric transformation during training.
Applied during training with scaling factors ranging from 0.8 to 1.2.

conclusions: Automated lesion masking significantly improves the diagnostic performance of mid-range deep learning models, specifically EfficientNetB0, which saw accuracy increase from 68.43% to 78.02% and macro-F1 score rise from 0.48 to 0.73.

High-performing models like Custom CNN (98.58% accuracy) and Xception (97.61% accuracy) showed negligible changes or slight performance drops after masking, likely because they already extracted sufficient features and did not rely on background context.
The performance improvements for EfficientNetB0 were statistically significant (p < 0.05), whereas the changes for Custom CNN (p = 0.116) and Xception (p = 0.414) were not statistically significant.

Lesion masking is particularly beneficial for detecting rare and malignant classes, such as Melanoma and Vascular Lesions, by reducing false negatives and removing distracting background artifacts like hair and ruler markings.
There was no significant difference in performance between using automated masks and manually refined masks, supporting the viability of automated preprocessing pipelines.

The study establishes that the impact of lesion masking is architecture-dependent, suggesting that masking is a critical preprocessing step for less complex models but may be redundant or detrimental for highly optimized architectures.
important_remarks: The study is published in a conference proceedings (PuneCon) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may limit the depth of methodological scrutiny compared to journal publications.

The Custom CNN achieved exceptionally high accuracy (98.58% unmasked), which is unusually high for the HAM10000 dataset and suggests potential data leakage or overfitting, as this performance is significantly higher than most literature benchmarks for baseline CNNs.
The Custom CNN architecture lacked regularization techniques such as dropout or batch normalization, increasing the risk of overfitting despite the high reported accuracy.
The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, limiting the generalizability of findings to other populations or dermatoscopic image sources without external validation.

The performance benefit of lesion masking is highly architecture-dependent; it significantly improved the mid-range EfficientNetB0 model but provided no significant benefit (and slight degradation) to high-performing models like Custom CNN and Xception, suggesting masking is not universally beneficial.

The paper notes that high-performing models (Custom CNN, Xception) may rely on background contextual cues (e.g., surrounding skin tone) for classification, which are removed by masking, potentially explaining the performance drop in benign classes like NV and BKL.
Synthetic upsampling was used to balance classes (targeting ~4,000 samples per category), which may introduce synthetic bias or over-represent minority classes compared to natural distribution.
The study does not report computational cost, inference time, or memory footprint, which are critical factors for clinical deployment of CAD systems.
The automated masks used for comparison were not validated against ground truth segmentation accuracy, assuming the provided pixel-level annotations were sufficient and error-free.
